content: {"position":"Microservices architecture is the right choice for this system — it provides independent deployability, technology diversity, and better fault isolation.","confidence":0.85,"key_arguments":["Independent deployment cycles reduce release risk","Teams can choose optimal technology per service","Fault isolation prevents cascade failures","Horizontal scaling per service optimizes resource usage"],"agrees_with_opponent":false,"counterpoints":[]}

{"position":"Monolithic architecture with modular boundaries is more appropriate — the team size and complexity do not justify microservices overhead.","confidence":0.80,"key_arguments":["Team of 5 developers cannot maintain 12+ services effectively","Shared database transactions are critical for data consistency","Deployment complexity of microservices exceeds current DevOps maturity","Modular monolith achieves separation without network overhead"],"agrees_with_opponent":false,"counterpoints":["Independent deployment is valuable but achievable with feature flags in a monolith","Technology diversity adds cognitive load disproportionate to team size"]}

{"position":"Microservices remain the superior long-term architecture — the initial overhead is an investment that pays off as the product scales.","confidence":0.75,"key_arguments":["Product roadmap indicates 3x team growth in 12 months","Event-driven architecture already partially implemented","Cloud-native tooling has reduced microservices operational burden significantly"],"agrees_with_opponent":false,"counterpoints":["Team size concern is temporary — hiring plan addresses this","Database consistency can be achieved with saga patterns"]}
